markdown.markdown:
Welcome to my blog! The only important information you really need to know is that I grew up in the beautiful [Meteora](https://www.wikiwand.com/en/Meteora). Other than this, my boring bio accumulates roughly 10 years of experience as a macroeconomist, mainly working in policy-making, economic research, and academia. Academically, I specialize in Fiscal Policy, Macroeconomic Theory, and Computational Economics. I am pretty much influenced by the theory and applications of [General Equilibrium with Incomplete Asset Markets](https://cowles.yale.edu/sites/default/files/files/pub/d09/d0919.pdf), which touches all aspects you can think of: from assessing capitalism to issues about wealth and income inequality, and obviously almost everything about finance and asset pricing theory or in discussing risk sharing in Eurozone. I am also strongly influenced by the works of [Peter Diamond](https://www.wikiwand.com/en/Peter_Diamond), [T. Sargent](https://www.wikiwand.com/en/Thomas_J._Sargent), and [O. Blanchard](https://www.wikiwand.com/en/Olivier_Blanchard). The combined influences of their work on my research helped me to build a solid knowledge around fiscal policy, public debt, and macroeconomic and finance theory. Computational Economics came as a byproduct of my research. I ended up discovering that I enjoy coding, programming and got reasonably good at it. I developed own ways of solving stochastic macroeconomic models with large state-space. Because of this strand of research, I later on discovered Machine Learning as it was related to some of the stuff I was working (See [here](https://web.stanford.edu/~maliars/Files/JME2021.pdf) for what I mean). I never managed to finish my academic work that I started at graduate school as I accidentally dropped out from academia and then had no courage to start over again. Now I don't feel much regret, but I do miss the time to be creative and think about interesting problems. Maybe this website is a reflection of this need. Since I cannot separate my quant and economist side, I decided to name this blog "_The Quant Economist_", but I mainly aim to write (infrequently) about macroeconomics, financial markets, and applied data science material. I believe that evidence based economics is pretty much data science, and data science is a good tool for better economics. Not to mention that data science (or Machine Learning) is the new econometrics. So, whenever I post about some applied data science material this is mostly to promote some good use-cases in macroeconomics or finance. Another reason that I like to use data science is because it helps to cut the noise from the financial press or the exposure to too much information. I admit I dislike most of the financial news. Most of the time the financial press writes too much and says too little, tends to unnecessarily complicate things, and recycles knowledge without any added value. After all, journalism is a business about entertainment not a search for truth. The trick is to stay away from most on what you read and try to develop your own process of making decisions. Here this process takes the form of exposing macroeconomic and finance theory and using data to better get a sense of the real world. This was also one of the reasons behind my [Python library](https://github.com/msh855/myPortfolioManagement) for asset management as it helps collect and analyse information in the way I see most fit for managing my savings as well as develop more intuition about the space of products that I might have to invest (PS: I still want to add a few extra features before I release it) Ah, one final plead. I encourage you to call me Safis. This is my informal name and how my family and friends call me. I hope you enjoy the content here.

 

 

 

 

***Disclaimer**: Nothing here should be construed as investment advice or representing the views of my current or past employers. Everything I write is my personal opinion and not recommendations to trade or invest. Investors should conduct their own due diligence and make their own decisions about investing. I may have direct or indirect long positions in stocks, indices, or mutual funds*.
